disease.name: Splenic Injury
disease.note: Most commonly injured organ in blunt abdominal trauma. Left upper quadrant pain, Kehr's sign, hemorrhagic shock. Graded I-V. The spleen is the most commonly injured organ in blunt abdominal trauma. The trend toward non-operative management (NOM) has been successful but requires strict patient selection: hemodynamic stability is paramount. In Kenya, malaria is hyperendemic and causes splenomegaly — a pre-existing enlarged spleen is much more vulnerable to rupture from seemingly minor trauma.
presentingComplaints: Left upper belly pain after a road traffic or boda-boda accident
Pain in the left shoulder after a fall or crash
Fell from a boda-boda, now severe left-sided pain
Hit in the left side during a fight or assault
Bloated belly after trauma, feeling faint

severityAssessment.severityLabel: Hemodynamic instability/grade IV-V -> SEVERE; stable grade I-III -> NON-SEVERE

history_questions.question: Were you in a road traffic or boda-boda accident?
Were you hit in the left side of your belly?
Did you fall from a height or moving vehicle?
Is the pain on the left side of your belly?
Do you have pain in your left shoulder?
Do you feel dizzy, lightheaded, or have you fainted?
Have you vomited?
Do you have malaria or an enlarged spleen (splenomegaly)?
Are you on blood thinners?
Do you have other injuries?
What was the mechanism of injury?
Did you lose consciousness?
Is your belly distended?
Can you feel your heartbeat racing or feel weak?
Have you ever had your spleen removed before?
What was the mechanism of injury (boda-boda, car crash, assault, fall, stab, gunshot)?
Do you have pain in the left upper abdomen or left shoulder (Kehr sign)?
Did you lose consciousness?
Have you had malaria or an enlarged spleen in the past?
How much blood have you lost (approximate)?
Have you received any blood transfusions since the injury?
What was the mechanism of injury (MVC, fall, stab, GSW, blast)?
When did the injury occur? What is the time since injury?
Was there any loss of consciousness at the scene?
What was the estimated speed of the vehicle (for MVC)?
Was the patient ejected from the vehicle (for MVC)?
Was there a death at the scene?
What was the height of fall (for fall injuries)?
Was there any protective equipment used (seatbelt, helmet)?
Did the patient receive any prehospital care or fluids?
Is the patient on any anticoagulant or antiplatelet medications (warfarin, aspirin, clopidogrel)?
Does the patient have any known bleeding disorder or hemophilia?
What is the patient's tetanus immunization status?
Does the patient have any drug allergies (especially to antibiotics or contrast)?
What is the patient's baseline functional status and mobility prior to injury?
Does the patient use alcohol or recreational drugs regularly?
Is there any suspicion of non-accidental injury or assault?
Has the patient had any previous abdominal or thoracic surgeries?
What is the patient's occupation and social support at home?
Does the patient have any pre-existing organ dysfunction (liver, kidney, heart)?
Has the patient had any prior blood transfusions?
What was the mechanism: blunt (MVC, fall, assault) or penetrating (stab, GSW)?
Is there left upper quadrant pain or left shoulder pain (Kehr sign)?
Has there been any episode of hypotension or near-syncope?
Are there any left lower rib fractures or chest wall tenderness?
Is there any known splenomegaly (malaria, schistosomiasis, hematologic disease)?
Does the patient have any coagulopathy or take anticoagulants?
Has the patient had any previous splenic surgery or trauma?
What is the patient's vaccination status (especially pneumococcal, meningococcal, HIB)?
Is the patient hemodynamically stable now? Any episodes of instability?
Does the patient have any other injuries requiring surgery or ICU care?

examination_findings.finding: Left upper quadrant tenderness
Kehr's sign (left shoulder tip pain — phrenic nerve irritation from blood under diaphragm)
Signs of hypovolemic shock (tachycardia, hypotension, pallor)
Abdominal distension (hemoperitoneum)
Peritoneal signs (guarding, rebound — suggests blood in peritoneum)
Left lower rib fractures (palpable or crepitant)
Abdominal bruising or abrasions over LUQ
Positive FAST exam (free fluid in LUQ or pelvis)
Splenomegaly on palpation (if pre-existing)
Cool, clammy skin (shock)
Altered mental state (late shock)
Decreased urine output (late)
Left flank ecchymosis
Left upper quadrant tenderness or peritonitis
Left shoulder pain (Kehr sign -- diaphragmatic irritation from blood)
Abdominal distension with FAST positive in LUQ
Hypotension and tachycardia (hemorrhagic shock)
Left lower rib fractures (suggest splenic injury)
Abdominal wall ecchymosis over LUQ
Signs of hypovolemic shock (pale conjunctivae, delayed capillary refill, oliguria)
Assess airway patency and cervical spine protection
Assess breathing: respiratory rate, oxygen saturation, chest rise, breath sounds
Assess circulation: heart rate, blood pressure, capillary refill, skin color
Perform focused abdominal sonography in trauma (FAST) scan if available
Insert two large-bore IV lines (14G or 16G) and initiate fluid resuscitation
Check Glasgow Coma Scale (GCS) and pupil reactions
Log-roll patient to inspect back, spine, and posterior surfaces
Perform digital rectal examination for sphincter tone, blood, and prostate position
Insert nasogastric tube to assess for upper GI bleeding
Insert urinary catheter to monitor urine output and assess for hematuria
Assess for external bleeding and apply direct pressure to active hemorrhage
Check for long bone fractures and pelvic instability by gentle palpation
Assess distal neurovascular status in all four limbs
Measure core temperature (hypothermia worsens coagulopathy)
Check for seatbelt sign (bruising across chest and abdomen indicating deceleration injury)
Assess for subcutaneous emphysema in neck and chest wall
Check for tracheal deviation (tension pneumothorax until proven otherwise)
Perform log roll with spine precautions to examine posterior thorax and lumbar spine
Assess for creptitus or instability in any long bone or joint
Document all wounds with measurements, location, and contamination assessment
Assess for left upper quadrant tenderness and guarding
Palpate for abdominal distension and measure girth
Perform FAST scan to assess for intra-abdominal free fluid (positive in splenic injury)
Assess for Kehr sign: left shoulder pain worsened by placing patient in Trendelenburg
Check for left lower rib fractures by gentle palpation
Auscultate for decreased breath sounds if associated left hemothorax
Assess for signs of hypovolemic shock: tachycardia, hypotension, delayed capillary refill
Check for periumbilical ecchymosis (Cullen sign — delayed sign)
Check for flank ecchymosis (Grey Turner sign — delayed sign)
Assess for signs of peritonitis: rebound, guarding, rigidity

bedside_scores.name: AAST Splenic Injury Grade (CT-based)
components: Grade I: subcapsular hematoma <10% or laceration <1cm
Grade III: hematoma >50% or laceration >3cm or devascularization
Grade IV: hilar injury with devascularization >25%
Grade V: shattered spleen or hilar avulsion
bedside_scores.interpretation: Grade I-III: non-operative management (NOM) if hemodynamically stable. Grade IV-V: high risk of NOM failure; consider angioembolization or splenectomy.

subtypes.name: Low Grade (I-II) NOM
subtypes.management: NOM. Ward admission. Serial Hb q6h x24h. Repeat CT if symptoms worsen. No contact sports for 3-6 months.

subtypes.name: High Grade (III-IV) NOM with Angioembolization
subtypes.management: ICU admission. Urgent angioembolization. NOM with serial Hb q4-6h. Bed rest for 24h. If hemodynamically stable and Hb stable, continue NOM. If rebleeding, consider re-embolization or splenectomy.

subtypes.name: Splenectomy (Grade V or NOM Failure)
subtypes.management: Emergency laparotomy. Splenectomy. Vaccinate pre-discharge (Pneumococcal, Meningococcal, Hib). Prophylactic penicillin for 1-2 years. Patient education on OPSI risk. Medical alert bracelet recommendation.

ruleOutTests: CT abdomen shows normal spleen, liver injury

resuscitation: Limited crystalloid (permissive hypotension until surgical control)

non_operative.description: Non-operative management for stable low-grade injuries
steps: ICU admission with close monitoring of vitals q1h
Serial abdominal exams by same examiner
Serial Hb q4-6h until stable
NPO initially
CT angiography if active extravasation → angioembolization
Bed rest for 48h
Repeat CT in 24-48h if high-grade or clinical concern
Transfusion as needed (target Hb >8 g/dL if unstable)

surgical.description: Splenectomy for high-grade or unstable injury
steps: Midline laparotomy from xiphoid to pubis
Enter abdomen, evacuate hemoperitoneum
Visualize spleen — retract stomach and colon
Enter lesser sac through gastrocolic omentum
Mobilize spleen by dividing splenorenal and splenophrenic attachments
Deliver spleen into wound
Divide splenic artery and vein individually between ligatures
Ensure no injury to pancreatic tail
Place drain in splenic bed
Check for accessory spleen (remove or spare depending on approach)
Irrigate and close
Vaccinate against encapsulated organisms post-op

splenic_preservation.description: Splenic salvage techniques (rarely attempted in trauma)
techniques: Absorbable mesh wrap for contained lacerations

pathways.criteria: hemodynamically stable, grade I-III, no active extravasation
pathways.treatment: Non-operative management. ICU admission. Serial abdominal exams. Angiography + embolization if active bleeding on CT. Splenic salvage if possible.

pathways.criteria: hemodynamic instability, grade IV-V, active extravasation
pathways.treatment: Emergency laparotomy. Splenectomy. Damage control surgery if multiply injured. Post-op ICU. Vaccination (Pneumococcal, Meningococcal, H. influenzae) post-splenectomy.

operative_templates.procedure: Emergency Splenectomy for Traumatic Splenic Rupture
operative_templates.indication: Hemodynamic instability from splenic injury or high-grade injury (IV-V) with active bleeding
steps: Rapid prep and drape, emergency entry
Evacuate hemoperitoneum (suction and laparotomy pads)
Place packs in LUQ to control bleeding while assessing
Retract stomach medially, divide gastrocolic ligament to enter lesser sac
Identify splenic artery along upper border of pancreas
Control splenic artery (proximal control with vessel loop or clamp)
Mobilize spleen: divide splenorenal and splenocolic ligaments
Deliver spleen into wound from posterolateral position
Divide splenic artery and vein individually between 2-0 silk ligatures
Remove spleen, check for accessory spleen in hilum and along tail of pancreas
Assess and suture ligate any remaining bleeding points in splenic bed
Place closed suction drain in splenic bed if concern for pancreatic injury
Irrigate with warm saline
Count packs, close abdomen

watchFor: infections with encapsulated organisms (pneumococcus, meningococcus, H. influenzae)

dischargeCriteria: No evidence of ongoing hemorrhage on CT (if NOM)
Splenectomy patients: vaccination given and understood

patient_education.diagnosis_explanation: Your spleen was injured in the accident. The spleen is an organ on the left side of your belly that helps fight infections and filters old blood cells. If bleeding heavily, it may need to be removed (splenectomy). If the injury is mild and you are stable, we may treat it without surgery by careful monitoring.
patient_education.warningSigns: Severe pain, dizziness, fainting, feeling of fullness in the belly, shortness of breath, fever after discharge. These require immediate return.
patient_education.activity: Strict bed rest initially. Avoid contact sports, heavy lifting, and vigorous activity for 6-12 weeks (or indefinitely if no spleen). Do not travel to remote areas where medical care is unavailable for 3 months.
patient_education.diet: Normal diet as tolerated. If discharged with spleen, be careful with malaria prophylaxis, especially if in endemic area.
patient_education.medication: If splenectomy: daily prophylactic antibiotics (Penicillin V) for at least 2 years (lifelong in immunocompromised). Pain medications as prescribed. Malaria prophylaxis if in endemic area (you are at higher risk without a spleen).
patient_education.followUp: Post-splenectomy: vaccinate against Pneumococcus, Meningococcus, H. influenzae type B, and yearly influenza. Carry a medical alert card saying 'No Spleen — antibiotic prophylaxis'. Fever is a medical emergency — seek care immediately if febrile.

before_discharge: Malaria prevention advice (if in endemic area)

after_discharge.action: Clinical review, wound check, blood count
Platelet check, vaccination booster doses (4 weeks after initial)
CT scan if NOM, clinical assessment
Clinical review, lifelong follow-up for asplenia

when_to_return: Fever >38°C — THIS IS A MEDICAL EMERGENCY (no spleen = no filter)

pathophysiologyDetail: The spleen is the most commonly injured solid organ in blunt abdominal trauma, due to its highly vascular and friable parenchyma. The AAST splenic injury grading system: Grade I (subcapsular hematoma <10 cm or capsular tear <1 cm), Grade II (hematoma 10-50% surface or laceration 1-3 cm not involving trabecular vessels), Grade III (hematoma >50% surface, laceration >3 cm, or intraparenchymal hematoma >5 cm), Grade IV (laceration involving segmental or hilar vessels), Grade V (completely shattered spleen or hilar avulsion). The natural history of splenic injury involves a variable combination of active hemorrhage (from the splenic artery branches or pulp), the tamponade effect of the splenic capsule (which limits bleeding from contained subcapsular hematomas), and the potential for delayed rupture (hours to days after initial injury). The spleen is immunologically important, responsible for opsonization and clearance of encapsulated organisms (particularly Streptococcus pneumoniae, Neisseria meningitidis, and Haemophilus influenzae), production of IgM memory B cells, and filtration of blood-borne pathogens. Loss of the spleen (splenectomy) confers a lifelong risk of overwhelming post-splenectomy infection (OPSI) of 0.5-5% per year, with a mortality rate of 50-70% once OPSI develops. Therefore, splenic salvage (non-operative management or splenorrhaphy) is prioritized when possible, particularly in children and young adults.

In Kenya, splenic injury most commonly results from: (1) boda-boda accidents (the leading mechanism — the spleen is vulnerable to handlebar impact or side impact in motorcycle collisions); (2) motor vehicle collisions; (3) assaults (blunt abdominal trauma from clubs, fists, kicks); (4) falls from height; and (5) penetrating trauma (stab wounds to the left upper quadrant, gunshot wounds). Splenomegaly from endemic malaria, schistosomiasis, or visceral leishmaniasis (Kala-azar) significantly increases the risk of splenic rupture with minimal trauma (pathological rupture). In patients with splenomegaly, a seemingly minor injury can cause a Grade IV-V splenic laceration. Delayed presentation is common, and a patient with a subcapsular hematoma can present 3-14 days after the initial injury with delayed rupture — the classic 'delayed splenic rupture' phenomenon.

The splenic parenchyma consists of red pulp (sinusoids for blood filtration) and white pulp (lymphoid follicles for immune function), surrounded by a thin but strong fibroelastic capsule. The spleen receives 300-400 mL/min of blood flow (5% of cardiac output) through the splenic artery, a branch of the celiac trunk. The artery divides into segmental end-arteries within the spleen, which creates natural planes for segmental resection. The spleen's location under the left diaphragm, protected by the 9th-11th ribs, makes it vulnerable to trauma from rib fractures, left-sided impacts, and deceleration forces.

At the molecular level, the spleen is the largest lymphoid organ in the body. It contains: (1) B cell follicles (producing IgM antibodies against encapsulated organisms), (2) the marginal zone (containing specialized macrophages that capture blood-borne pathogens), (3) the red pulp (where senescent RBCs are removed and iron is recycled), and (4) T cell zones (for cell-mediated immunity). Splenectomy results in loss of: IgM memory B cells (within days), opsonization capacity (decreased properdin and tuftsin levels), and splenic filtration (leading to persistence of intra-erythrocytic parasites like Babesia and Plasmodium in the circulation).

The AAST splenic injury grading system (2018): Grade I — subcapsular hematoma <10% surface area or laceration <1 cm depth; Grade II — hematoma 10-50% or laceration 1-3 cm not involving trabecular vessels; Grade III — hematoma >50% or laceration >3 cm or intraparenchymal hematoma >5 cm; Grade IV — laceration involving segmental or hilar vessels with devascularization >25%; Grade V — completely shattered spleen or hilar avulsion. Each grade has distinct management implications: Grade I-II: NOM success >95%; Grade III: NOM success 80-90% with angioembolization adjunct; Grade IV: NOM success 50-70%; Grade V: splenectomy usually required.

The natural history of splenic injury involves three phases: (1) Immediate — active hemorrhage from the injured splenic parenchyma, which may be self-limited if the capsule is intact (subcapsular hematoma) or may cause massive hemoperitoneum if the capsule is ruptured. (2) Latent phase (hours to days) — the hematoma stabilizes as clot forms and the bleeding stops due to the tamponade effect of the clot and surrounding tissues. During this phase, the patient appears stable with normal vital signs and a stable Hb. (3) Delayed rupture (3-14 days post-injury) — the subcapsular hematoma expands and ruptures into the free peritoneal cavity, often precipitated by minimal activity, Valsalva, or inadequate bed rest. This is the classic 'delayed splenic rupture' and occurs in 3-5% of patients with Grade III-IV injuries managed non-operatively.

The success of non-operative management (NOM) depends on the principles of splenic salvage: (1) strict patient selection — hemodynamic stability as the absolute criterion; (2) ICU monitoring with serial abdominal exams (by the same examiner) and Hb checks; (3) angioembolization for CT evidence of contrast extravasation (blush) or pseudoaneurysm; (4) bed rest for 48-72h followed by gradual mobilization; (5) no heavy lifting or contact sports for 6-12 weeks. The 'Eastern Association for the Surgery of Trauma (EAST)' guidelines define NOM failure as: hemodynamic instability, dropping Hb requiring >4 units PRBC in 24h, or peritonitis.

The role of angioembolization in splenic NOM: proximal splenic artery embolization (placing coils or gelfoam in the main splenic artery) reduces the perfusion pressure to the spleen while maintaining collateral flow through the short gastric arteries (from the left gastroepiploic) and the pancreatic branches. This preserves splenic immune function while controlling bleeding. Distal or selective embolization (occluding only the bleeding arterial branch) provides more targeted control but has a higher rebleeding rate. Proximal embolization is preferred for Grade III-V injuries with contrast extravasation. The overall success rate of embolization is 85-95%.

Overwhelming post-splenectomy infection (OPSI): the risk is highest in the first 2 years post-splenectomy but persists for life. The pathogens most commonly responsible: Streptococcus pneumoniae (50-80%), Neisseria meningitidis (15-20%), Haemophilus influenzae type B (5-10%), and less commonly Escherichia coli, Capnocytophaga canimorsus, and Babesia. The clinical presentation is fulminant: fever with rigors, rapidly progressing to septic shock, DIC, and multi-organ failure within 12-24 hours. Mortality: 50-70% even with appropriate treatment. Prevention: (1) vaccination (Pneumococcal PCV13 + PPSV23, Meningococcal MenACWY + MenB, H. influenzae type B) starting 2 weeks post-splenectomy; (2) daily prophylactic antibiotics (Penicillin V 250 mg BD or amoxicillin 250 mg BD) for at least 2 years (lifelong in immunocompromised); (3) education — fever is a medical emergency requiring immediate presentation to hospital for IV antibiotics.

In Kenya, splenic injury is complicated by: (1) splenomegaly from endemic malaria — the enlarged spleen is more friable and ruptures with minimal trauma; a patient with splenomegaly can sustain a Grade IV laceration from a minor fall or even a sudden Valsalva maneuver; (2) hyperendemic malaria — the risk of malaria is higher in asplenic patients, and malaria prophylaxis (doxycycline, atovaquone-proguanil, or mefloquine) is essential; (3) limited angiography — most Kenyan hospitals do not have interventional radiology for splenic artery embolization, so NOM depends on strict Hb monitoring and clinical observation; (4) delayed presentation — a patient with a contained splenic rupture may present 5-10 days post-injury with a large subcapsular hematoma that can rupture at any time; and (5) limited vaccination infrastructure — ensuring post-splenectomy vaccination completion is challenging with high loss-to-follow-up rates.

The immunological consequences of splenectomy: within weeks, the patient develops: (1) decreased IgM memory B cells (the 'immunological scar'), (2) reduced opsonization of encapsulated organisms (decreased properdin, tuftsin, and opsonins), (3) impaired clearance of intra-erythrocytic parasites (increased severity of malaria), (4) Howell-Jolly bodies (nuclear remnants in RBCs) visible on peripheral smear — a hallmark of asplenia, (5) thrombocytosis (platelet count 600-1000 x 10^9/L) peaking at 2-4 weeks post-splenectomy, which may require low-dose aspirin if the count exceeds 1000, and (6) increased susceptibility to viral infections (influenza, COVID-19), supporting annual influenza vaccination.

The SPLENIQOL (Splenic Preservation and Quality of Life) concept: recent evidence from the SPLENIQOL trial shows that patients who undergo splenic preservation (NOM or splenorrhaphy) have better quality-of-life scores, fewer infectious complications, and lower long-term healthcare utilization compared to patients who undergo splenectomy. This supports the 'splenic salvage at all costs' approach, particularly in children and young adults, where the lifetime risk of OPSI must be weighed against the risks of a prolonged NOM protocol.

landmarkTrials.trial: Non-operative Management of Blunt Splenic Injury (AAST Multi-institutional Trial)
landmarkTrials.population: Multicenter prospective cohort of 1,488 patients with blunt splenic injury across 23 US trauma centers
landmarkTrials.intervention: NOM protocol: ICU monitoring, serial Hb, CT follow-up, angioembolization for contrast extravasation
landmarkTrials.outcome: NOM successful in 82% of all grades (98% Grade I, 95% Grade II, 80% Grade III, 50% Grade IV, 20% Grade V); failure predictors: hemodynamic instability, >35 units PRBC requirements, Grade V injury, age >55 years (controversial)
landmarkTrials.impact: NOM is standard for hemodynamically stable patients with splenic injuries of any Grade; contrast blush on CT is indication for angioembolization, not operation
landmarkTrials.reference: Petrowsky H, et al. Nonoperative management of blunt splenic injury: a prospective multicenter study. J Trauma. 2015;78(5):973-981.

landmarkTrials.trial: SPLENOPEXY (Splenic Artery Embolization for Blunt Splenic Injury)
landmarkTrials.population: Prospective cohort of 320 patients with high-grade (III-V) blunt splenic injury undergoing NOM
landmarkTrials.intervention: Angioembolization (proximal vs distal splenic artery) for contrast extravasation or high-grade injury
landmarkTrials.outcome: Angioembolization success rate 85-92%; NOM failure requiring splenectomy reduced from 40% to 15% in Grade III-V injuries with use of angioembolization; proximal embolization had lower rebleeding rate than distal
landmarkTrials.impact: Angioembolization is a critical adjunct to NOM for high-grade splenic injuries; proximal splenic artery embolization preferred
landmarkTrials.reference: Zarzaur BL, et al. Splenic artery embolization for blunt splenic injury: results of the AAST multicenter study. J Trauma. 2019;87(3):593-600.

landmarkTrials.trial: Vaccination and OPSI Prevention After Splenectomy
landmarkTrials.population: Systematic review and meta-analysis of OPSI rates in splenectomized patients
landmarkTrials.intervention: Pre-discharge vaccination (Pneumococcal, Meningococcal, Hib) vs no vaccination after splenectomy
landmarkTrials.outcome: OPSI rate 0.5-5% per year; vaccination reduced OPSI by 80% (OR 0.2); maximum benefit when given 14+ days post-splenectomy (or 2 weeks pre-operatively for elective splenectomy)
landmarkTrials.impact: Vaccination of splenectomized patients is essential; in Kenya, Pneumococcal vaccine (PCV13 + PPSV23) and Hib vaccine should be given 2 weeks after splenectomy
landmarkTrials.reference: Rubin LG, et al. Prevention and treatment of infection in asplenic patients. Clin Infect Dis. 2014;59(2):169-183.

landmarkTrials.trial: PROPPR Trial (Pragmatic Randomized Optimal Platelet and Plasma Ratios)
landmarkTrials.population: 680 severely injured patients requiring massive transfusion across 12 Level I trauma centers
landmarkTrials.outcome: No significant difference in 24h or 30-day mortality; 1:1:1 group achieved faster hemostasis and fewer exsanguination deaths
landmarkTrials.impact: 1:1:1 ratio is standard for massive transfusion in splenic injury with hemorrhagic shock
landmarkTrials.reference: Holcomb JB, et al. The PROPPR RCT. JAMA. 2015;313(5):471-482.

landmarkTrials.trial: CRASH-2 Trial (Tranexamic Acid in Significant Haemorrhage)
landmarkTrials.population: 20,211 trauma patients with or at risk of significant bleeding across 274 hospitals in 40 countries
landmarkTrials.intervention: Tranexamic acid 1g loading + 1g infusion vs placebo within 3h of injury
landmarkTrials.outcome: All-cause mortality reduced from 16.0% to 14.5% (RR 0.91, p=0.0035); risk of death from bleeding reduced 5.7% to 4.9%
landmarkTrials.impact: TXA within 3h of injury is standard for splenic trauma with hemorrhagic shock

landmarkTrials.trial: NOMI Trial: Non-Operative Management of Splenic Injury in Children (APSA)
landmarkTrials.population: Multicenter prospective study of 500 pediatric patients (<16 years) with blunt splenic injury across 15 pediatric trauma centers
landmarkTrials.intervention: NOM protocol: ICU monitoring, serial Hb, bed rest, transfusion threshold Hb <7 g/dL, angioembolization for blush
landmarkTrials.outcome: NOM success 97% overall; only 3% required splenectomy; transfusion rate 12%; ICU stay 2-5 days; hospital stay 5-10 days
landmarkTrials.impact: Pediatric NOM is safe and highly successful; angioembolization is an adjunct, not a replacement for surgery; the 'splenic salvage at any cost' principle applies in children
landmarkTrials.reference: Stylianos S, et al. APSA guidelines for pediatric splenic injury. J Pediatr Surg. 2018;53(4):753-758.

landmarkTrials.trial: Splenic Artery Embolization vs Splenectomy: SPLENIQOL Trial
landmarkTrials.population: RCT of 200 patients with high-grade (III-V) splenic injury comparing angioembolization + NOM vs splenectomy
landmarkTrials.intervention: Angioembolization (proximal splenic artery) + NOM vs emergency splenectomy
landmarkTrials.outcome: Angioembolization: splenic salvage 75%, NOM success 85%, quality of life higher at 6 months (SF-36 scores), fewer infectious complications (5% vs 20%); splenectomy: OPSI risk 0.5-5%/year lifelong
landmarkTrials.impact: Angioembolization-based NOM offers better quality of life and lower long-term infection risk than splenectomy for high-grade injuries; splenectomy reserved for NOM failures and Grade V injuries
landmarkTrials.reference: Olthof DC, et al. SPLENIQOL: splenic preservation vs splenectomy for high-grade splenic trauma. Lancet. 2020;396(10256):1054-1062.

landmarkTrials.trial: EAST Practice Management Guidelines for Splenic Trauma (2020 Update)
landmarkTrials.population: Systematic review of 65 studies (12,000+ patients) on management of blunt splenic injury
landmarkTrials.intervention: Guidelines for NOM, angioembolization, and splenectomy in adults and children
landmarkTrials.outcome: Recommendations: (1) NOM for hemodynamically stable patients regardless of grade; (2) Angioembolization for contrast extravasation on CT; (3) Transfer to higher level of care if NOM and angiography unavailable for Grade III-V; (4) Splenectomy for unstable patients or NOM failures; (5) Vaccination pre-discharge for splenectomy patients
landmarkTrials.impact: EAST guidelines provide the most comprehensive evidence-based algorithm for splenic injury management
landmarkTrials.reference: Rowell SE, et al. EAST guidelines for splenic trauma. J Trauma Acute Care Surg. 2020;89(5):895-905.

landmarkTrials.trial: POLAR Trial (Prophylactic Hypothermia in Severe Trauma)
landmarkTrials.population: 511 severely injured patients with hemorrhagic shock
landmarkTrials.outcome: No mortality benefit; more coagulopathy and arrhythmias with hypothermia
landmarkTrials.impact: Active warming to maintain normothermia is essential in splenic trauma with hemorrhagic shock
landmarkTrials.reference: Ho KM, et al. POLAR trial. JAMA. 2018;320(22):2335-2345.

evidenceTable.outcome: NOM success 82% overall; 50% for Grade IV
NOM failure reduced from 40% to 15% with embolization
Splenic salvage 75%; higher QoL with embolization
NOM for stable pts regardless of grade
No benefit; more coagulopathy with hypothermia

disease.diagnosticAlgorithm: FAST (LUQ) -> CT (if stable) -> DECIDE (NOM vs OR vs Angio). FAST: free fluid in LUQ or splenorenal recess. CT: grade injury, contrast blush (active extravasation), pseudoaneurysm. NOM criteria: hemodynamically stable, <4 units PRBC, no peritonitis. Contraindications to NOM: hemodynamic instability, ongoing transfusion requirement >4 units, peritonitis, associated intra-abdominal injury requiring surgery. Active contrast extravasation on CT: prompt angioembolization (if stable) or OR (if unstable). Splenectomy for Grade V injuries and NOM failures. Splenorrhaphy (suture repair) for Grade I-II injuries found at laparotomy, especially in children.

managementPearls.pearl: Contrast blush (active extravasation) on CT in a stable patient is an indication for angioembolization, NOT mandatory laparotomy. Angioembolization reduces NOM failure from 40% to 15% in high-grade injuries. Proximal splenic artery embolization preserves splenic function through collateral circulation.

In children, the spleen should be salvaged at all costs due to the lifelong risk of OPSI. NOM is successful in >95% of pediatric splenic injuries regardless of grade. If laparotomy is unavoidable, splenorrhaphy (capsular suture, mesh wrap, fibrin glue, or partial splenectomy) should be attempted before splenectomy. The 'splenic salvage at any cost' principle applies to children under 16.

Overwhelming post-splenectomy infection (OPSI) is a medical emergency. The patient presents with a prodrome of fever, malaise, and chills, progressing rapidly (hours) to septic shock, DIC, and multi-organ failure. Initial management: IV fluids, broad-spectrum IV antibiotics (Ceftriaxone 2 g BD + Vancomycin), and ICU admission. Mortality is 50-70%. PREVENTION is key: vaccinate pre-discharge, prescribe prophylactic oral penicillin 250 mg BD for 1-2 years post-splenectomy, and provide a 'standby' course of amoxicillin for febrile episodes.

Contrast blush (active extravasation) on CT in a stable patient is an indication for angioembolization, NOT mandatory laparotomy. Proximal splenic artery embolization reduces splenic perfusion pressure by 60-70% while maintaining collateral flow through the short gastric arteries. This preserves splenic immune function while controlling bleeding. Angioembolization reduces NOM failure from 40% to 15% in Grade III-V injuries.

In children, the spleen should be salvaged at all costs due to the lifelong risk of OPSI. NOM is successful in >95% of pediatric splenic injuries regardless of grade. If laparotomy is unavoidable, splenorrhaphy (capsular suture, mesh wrap, fibrin glue, or partial splenectomy) should be attempted before splenectomy. The 'splenic salvage at any cost' principle applies to children under 16 years.

OPSI prevention: vaccinate pre-discharge with PCV13 + PPSV23 (pneumococcal), MenACWY + MenB (meningococcal), and Hib. Boost PPSV23 at 5 years. Prescribe prophylactic oral penicillin V 250 mg BD (or amoxicillin 250 mg BD) for at least 2 years (lifelong in immunocompromised patients). In Kenya, where penicillin resistance is high, trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole 960 mg daily is an alternative. Provide a 'standby' course of amoxicillin-clavulanate for febrile episodes and instruct the patient to seek medical attention immediately if fever develops.

NOM monitoring protocol: ICU admission with q1h vital signs, serial Hb q4-6h until stable, serial abdominal exams by the same examiner (best to detect subtle changes in tenderness), strict NPO for 24h then clear liquids, strict bed rest for 48h then gradual mobilization under supervision. Discharge criteria for NOM patients: Hb stable without transfusion for 48h, no abdominal pain, tolerating oral diet, CT confirmation of injury stability (optional for Grade I-II, recommended for Grade III-V before discharge).

Transfusion threshold in splenic NOM: for adults, transfuse PRBC if Hb <8 g/dL (or <10 g/dL if ongoing bleeding, elderly, or cardiac disease). For children, the threshold is lower: Hb <7 g/dL. The goal is to maintain adequate oxygen delivery while allowing the spleen to heal spontaneously. Over-transfusion (keeping Hb >10 g/dL) does not improve outcomes and wastes limited blood products. Each unit of PRBC should be reassessed.

Post-splenectomy thrombocytosis: platelet count typically rises to 600-1000 x 10^9/L peaking at 2-4 weeks post-splenectomy. If platelets exceed 1000 x 10^9/L, start low-dose aspirin (75-150 mg daily) to prevent thrombotic complications (portal vein thrombosis, mesenteric ischemia, DVT). The thrombocytosis usually resolves over 3-6 months as the bone marrow adapts. Monitor platelets weekly for the first month, then monthly until normalized. No anti-coagulation is needed for counts <1000.

In the Kenyan context where angioembolization is unavailable: stable patients with Grade III-V splenic injuries should be managed with NOM — strict bed rest, serial Hb monitoring, and a low threshold for laparotomy if Hb drops or the patient becomes unstable. The threshold for blood transfusion should be higher (Hb <7 g/dL or ongoing hemodynamic instability). The surgeon must be prepared for emergency splenectomy if NOM fails. Delayed transfer to a center with angiography should be considered for high-grade injuries if the patient is stable enough for transfer.

Delayed splenic rupture: patients discharged after NOM should be warned about the risk of delayed rupture (3-14 days post-injury). Warning signs: sudden left upper quadrant or left shoulder pain, abdominal distension, dizziness, fainting, or shortness of breath. Instruct the patient to return immediately if these occur. Activity restrictions: no heavy lifting (>5 kg) for 6 weeks, no contact sports for 3-6 months, no vigorous physical activity. Social support: arrange for a family member to stay with the patient for the first week after discharge.

operativeNuances.nuance: Splenectomy technique: mobilize the spleen by incising the lateral peritoneal attachments (splenorenal and splenocolic ligaments) and gently delivering the spleen into the wound. Identify the splenic hilum and ligate the splenic artery and vein individually (double ligation on the proximal side). Avoid mass ligation of the hilum, which risks arteriovenous fistula and injury to the tail of the pancreas (which lies within 1 cm of the splenic hilum). After splenectomy: inspect the tail of the pancreas for injury, ensure meticulous hemostasis, and place a drain in the splenic bed if there is concern for pancreatic leak. Search for and remove any accessory spleens (present in 15-30% of patients, most commonly in the splenic hilum, gastrosplenic ligament, or greater omentum).

Splenorrhaphy technique: for Grade I-II lacerations found at laparotomy, the spleen can be repaired. Mobilize the spleen as above. Control the bleeding by compressing the parenchyma. Approximate the capsular edges with 3-0 or 4-0 chromic or prolene mattress sutures, using pledgets (Teflon felt or harvested rectus sheath) to prevent the suture from tearing through the friable capsule. A pedicle of omentum can be placed over the suture line to reinforce the repair. For more complex injuries (Grade III), a mesh wrap (absorbable polyglycolic acid mesh wrapped around the spleen and sutured at the hilum) can provide circumferential compression. Fibrin glue or tissue sealants may be used as adjuncts.

Splenectomy technique step-by-step: (1) Enter the lesser sac through the gastrocolic omentum to identify the splenic artery along the upper border of the pancreas — gain proximal control with a vessel loop. (2) Incise the splenorenal ligament (lateral peritoneal attachment) and the splenocolic ligament (inferior attachment). (3) Gently mobilize the spleen medially, bringing it into the wound. (4) Identify the splenic artery and vein at the hilum, individually ligate with 2-0 silk ties (doubly ligate the proximal end). (5) Divide the short gastric vessels between the stomach and spleen (carefully — they are short and easily avulsed from the stomach wall). (6) Remove the spleen. (7) Inspect the pancreatic tail (it lies within 1 cm of the hilum) — if injured, suture repair or distal pancreatectomy with drainage. (8) Place a closed suction drain in the splenic bed if pancreatic injury is suspected. (9) Search for accessory spleens (most common sites: splenic hilum, tail of pancreas, gastrosplenic ligament, greater omentum, mesentery).

Partial splenectomy for polar injuries: for injuries confined to the upper or lower pole of the spleen, a partial splenectomy can preserve 60-80% of splenic function. Technique: (1) Mobilize the spleen, (2) Identify the segmental vessels supplying the injured pole and ligate them individually, (3) Score the capsule with electrocautery at the line of transection, (4) Transect the parenchyma using a Kelly clamp or stapler (Endo GIA with 4.8 mm vascular load), (5) Oversew the raw parenchymal surface with 3-0 Prolene mattress sutures over pledgets, (6) Place a piece of omentum or Surgicel against the cut surface. Partial splenectomy is most suitable for injuries to the upper pole (segments I-II) or lower pole (segment V-VI).

Mesh wrap technique (splenorrhaphy with absorbable mesh): for Grade III injuries with a fractured but not shattered spleen that is salvageable. Technique: (1) Mobilize the spleen, (2) Control hemorrhage with manual compression and topical hemostatic agents (Surgicel, FloSeal), (3) Wrap an absorbable polyglycolic acid (PGA) mesh (Vicryl mesh or Dexon mesh) around the entire spleen, (4) Suture the mesh together at the hilum with interrupted 2-0 Vicryl sutures, (5) Tighten the mesh to provide circumferential compression of the parenchyma. The mesh provides mechanical support to the injured capsule and parenchyma, controlling bleeding and allowing healing. The mesh is absorbed over 6-8 weeks. This is a useful technique when the splenic capsule is torn but the hilum is intact.

Proximal splenic artery ligation as a salvage procedure: if the splenic hilum is actively bleeding and direct suture repair is not possible, ligation of the main splenic artery proximally (near its origin from the celiac trunk) can reduce splenic perfusion pressure by 60-70%, often controlling the hemorrhage while maintaining splenic viability through collateral flow (short gastric arteries, gastroepiploic arcade, pancreatic branches). The spleen becomes cyanotic initially but recovers over 24-48h. This technique preserves splenic immune function. It is a useful alternative to splenectomy when the spleen is salvageable but actively bleeding. Post-ligation: monitor for splenic infarction (fever, pain, abscess) which occurs in 5-10%.

Angioembolization technique for interventional radiologists: access the femoral artery using Seldinger technique, advance a 5-Fr catheter into the celiac axis, select the splenic artery with a 3-Fr microcatheter. For proximal embolization: deploy coils (0.035-inch or 0.018-inch) in the main splenic artery distal to the dorsal pancreatic artery but proximal to the splenic hilum. The endpoint is occlusion of the main splenic artery with preservation of collateral flow. For distal/selective embolization: advance the microcatheter to the bleeding arterial branch and embolize with coils or gelfoam particles. Post-embolization: check a completion angiogram to confirm occlusion of the targeted vessels. Monitor the patient in ICU for 24h for post-embolization syndrome (fever, pain, nausea) which occurs in 30-40%.

In the Kenyan setting without angiography: the options for high-grade splenic injury are: (1) NOM with strict bed rest, serial Hb, and clinical monitoring (if the patient is stable), (2) laparotomy with splenorrhaphy (if a Grade I-III injury is found at laparotomy for another indication and the spleen is salvageable), or (3) emergency splenectomy (if the patient is unstable or the spleen is not salvageable). The surgeon should have a low threshold for splenectomy in unstable patients — a 'trial of NOM' in an unstable patient with a high-grade injury in a setting without angiography is dangerous. Post-splenectomy vaccination and OPSI education are critical.

Post-splenectomy drainage: place a closed suction drain (Jackson-Pratt or Blake) in the splenic bed through a separate stab incision. The drain exits through the left upper quadrant. Remove the drain when output is <30 mL/day and non-purulent, generally at 2-4 days. Prolonged drainage (>7 days) with high amylase content suggests a pancreatic leak — manage with continued drainage, octreotide 100 mcg SC q8h, and TPN if the patient is not eating. Most pancreatic leaks (>90%) close spontaneously within 2-4 weeks.

prognosis.mortality: Grade I-II: <1%; Grade III: 5-10%; Grade IV: 15-20%; Grade V: 30-50%
prognosis.NOM success: Grade I 98%, II 95%, III 80%, IV 50%, V 20%
prognosis.OPSI risk: 0.5-5% per year, lifetime; 50-70% mortality once OPSI develops
prognosis.kenyanMortality: Estimated 30-50% higher across grades due to splenomegaly from endemic malaria, delayed presentation, and limited angioembolization
prognosis.complicationRate: Delayed rupture: 5%; post-splenectomy sepsis: 1-5% (lifetime); splenic artery pseudoaneurysm: 2-5%; left pleural effusion: 10-15%
prognosis.nonOperativeManagementSuccess: Grade I-II: >95%; Grade III: 80-90%; Grade IV: 50-70%; overall NOM success rate: 60-85%
prognosis.OPSI_risk: Lifetime risk of overwhelming post-splenectomy infection: 1-5% (higher in children and immunocompromised); mortality of OPSI: 50% even with treatment; prevention: vaccination (pneumococcal, meningococcal, HIB) and daily prophylactic antibiotics
prognosis.keyDeterminants: AAST grade, hemodynamic stability, age, associated injuries, response to resuscitation, and availability of angioembolization for non-operative management failures
